pmos

Product Strategy & Vision — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Strategy. "Strategy" is the most abused word in product, and most of what gets called product strategy is a roadmap, a goal, or a slogan wearing strategy's clothes. This primer maps the canon rather than arguing a house style, and the canon is unusually coherent once you line the camps up. The spine is Richard Rumelt's kernel — a strategy is a diagnosis of the central challenge, a guiding policy for dealing with it, and coherent action to carry it out — paired with his anatomy of bad strategy. On top of that sit the structural frameworks: Lafley and Martin's five-choice cascade and Roger Martin's insistence that where-to-play and how-to-win are inseparable; Gibson Biddle's DHM test; Hamilton Helmer's 7 Powers for durable advantage; and Simon Wardley's maps for situational awareness. The product-specific translation comes from Melissa Perri (strategy is a system, not a plan), Marty Cagan and Roman Pichler on the vision→strategy→roadmap hierarchy, and the outcomes camp — Teresa Torres, Josh Seiden, the North Star Framework, and Amazon's working-backwards discipline. Where the camps genuinely disagree — how prescriptive a strategy should be, whether OKRs or a North Star should anchor it, top-down vision versus emergent — this primer flags it rather than picking a winner. Provenance: practitioner-canon synthesis. 15 verified sources.

What strategy actually is — Rumelt's kernel

The most durable definition of strategy in the canon is also the least glamorous, and it comes from outside product entirely: Richard Rumelt's Good Strategy / Bad Strategy. Rumelt's central move is to strip strategy down to what he calls the kernel, and as the kernel goes, strategy is three things and only three things working together: a diagnosis that names the nature of the challenge by "comprehending the situation and identifying the biggest barriers," a guiding policy for dealing with that challenge that works "Like the guardrails on a highway," and a set of coherent actions coordinated to carry the policy out. The power of the kernel is that it disqualifies most of what organizations call strategy. A revenue target is not a diagnosis. A list of five priorities is not a guiding policy. A roadmap of unrelated bets is not coherent action.

The guardrail metaphor is worth sitting with, because it captures what a good guiding policy does and does not do. Guardrails do not tell you how fast to drive or which exit to take; they rule out the catastrophic moves and channel everything else toward the destination. A guiding policy works the same way — it is a general approach that makes thousands of downstream decisions easier without scripting any of them. That is why a strategy you can fully express as a Gantt chart is almost certainly not a strategy; it has collapsed the guiding policy into the actions and skipped the diagnosis entirely.

Lenny Rachitsky's distillation lands on the same load-bearing point and sharpens it for practitioners: "the most common cause of bad strategy is a weak diagnosis." This is the part product teams skip most reliably. It is far more comfortable to leap to the guiding policy ("we'll win on usability") or straight to actions ("ship these twelve features") than to do the unglamorous work of stating, plainly and unflinchingly, what the actual obstacle is. But a guiding policy aimed at the wrong challenge is worse than no policy, because it coordinates the whole organization's effort in the wrong direction. The diagnosis is where the leverage is, and it is where the courage is required.

Bad strategy, and how to recognize it

Rumelt's second contribution is as useful as the kernel: a field guide to bad strategy, which he treats not as the absence of strategy but as an active, recognizable thing with a signature. Per the four hallmarks, bad strategy shows up as fluff (gibberish dressed as strategic concepts — "fluff is a restatement of the obvious, combined with a generous sprinkling of buzzwords"), failure to face the challenge (a document that never actually names what is hard), mistaking goals for strategy (a wish list of targets with no theory of how to reach them), and bad strategic objectives (a scatter of disconnected initiatives, or objectives that don't address the real obstacle). Once you have these four names, you cannot un-see them in your own decks.

The most common in product is mistaking goals for strategy. "Grow ARR 40%, improve NPS to 50, expand into two new segments" is a perfectly respectable set of goals and contains no strategy whatsoever — it states where you want to arrive and is silent on the central challenge in the way. Rumelt's point is not that goals are bad; it is that a goal becomes a substitute for thinking the moment it is presented as the strategy. The tell is that you could hand the goals to any competitor and they would want the same things; what would differ is the diagnosis and the guiding policy, which is exactly the part that has gone missing.

Fluff is the second-most common, and it is dangerous precisely because it feels like progress. A vision statement assembled from "customer-centric," "platform," "ecosystem," and "world-class" can pass three rounds of review without anyone noticing it makes no falsifiable claim. The discipline Rumelt forces is to ask of every strategy sentence: could the opposite reasonably be argued? If no competitor would ever state the inverse ("we will be customer-hostile and low-quality"), the sentence is fluff and carries no information. Good strategy, by contrast, is full of choices a sane competitor might have made differently — that is what makes it a strategy and not a platitude.

Vision, strategy, and roadmap — three different things

The single most useful clarification a product leader can install is the distinction between vision, strategy, and roadmap, because conflating them is the source of an enormous amount of wasted argument. Marty Cagan draws the first line cleanly: "Vision is describing your destination," typically "five to 10 years out," while product strategy is "a living thing" that you keep "revisiting it every quarter." The vision is stable and inspirational; the strategy is mutable and analytical. Treating the vision like a strategy makes it brittle (you keep rewriting your north star); treating the strategy like a vision makes it vague (you never revisit it).

Roman Pichler formalizes the relationship as a hierarchy — vision → strategy → roadmap → backlog — in which, as you descend, "the decisions you take become more specific" and the time frames compress "from five to ten years covered by the vision" down to a backlog spanning "the next few months." The vision "describes the product's purpose, the ultimate reason for creating it, and the positive change it should bring about." The strategy "communicates the approach chosen to realise the vision and to make the product successful" through choices about which customer needs to address, which market to target, what makes the product stand out, and what the business goals are. The roadmap is then the more concrete, time-bound expression of that strategy — not its replacement.

The practical consequence is a diagnostic you can run on any artifact handed to you as "the strategy." If it is a dated list of features, it is a roadmap and you should ask what strategy produced it. If it is an inspirational paragraph about changing the world, it is a vision and you should ask what this quarter's strategy is for getting there. The strategy is the missing middle layer most often — the connective tissue that explains why these features, in this order, for this market — and its absence is exactly why so many roadmaps feel like a backlog with dates rather than a coherent bet.

What good product strategy is — a system, not a plan

The most quoted line in modern product strategy comes from Melissa Perri, and it is a direct attack on the roadmap-as-strategy habit: product strategy is "a system of achievable goals and visions that work together to align the team around desirable outcomes," and "when we treat a product strategy like a plan, it will almost always fail." Her distinction between a system and a plan is the crux. A plan is a fixed sequence that assumes the world holds still; a system is a decision-making framework that keeps working as the world changes, because it tells you how to choose, not what to do on Tuesday.

This connects directly to the failure mode Perri is most associated with — the build trap. In Escaping the Build Trap, the trap is "when organizations measure their success by outputs rather than outcomes," and the antidote is a product strategy that is "not a plan but a framework that helps to make decisions" and that "connects vision to economic outcomes." Perri's product-led organization "is a culture that organizes around outcomes" rather than feature throughput. The strategic point hiding inside what sounds like a process critique is this: if your strategy is a feature plan, you will measure feature delivery, and you will get a feature factory. The shape of the strategy determines the shape of the metrics, which determines the behavior.

What makes a product strategy a real system rather than a slogan is that it is deployable — a team several layers down can use it to make a local decision without escalating. That is the same property Rumelt's guiding policy has, and the same one Cagan calls strategic context. The test for whether you have a strategy or just a plan: hand it to a squad lead facing a tradeoff you didn't anticipate, and see whether it helps them choose. If it only tells them what was already on the roadmap, it is a plan; if it tells them how to decide, it is a strategy.

Playing to Win — strategy as an integrated set of choices

The most widely adopted strategy framework in product leadership is Lafley and Martin's Playing to Win, and its appeal is that it turns the abstract idea of strategy into five concrete questions. Per the framework, a strategy is "a coordinated and integrated set of five choices: a winning aspiration, where to play, how to win, core capabilities, and management systems" — and the framing sentence that cuts through the fog is that "strategy is about making specific choices to win in the marketplace." Strategy, in this view, is not analysis or planning; it is choosing, and specifically choosing what not to do.

The deepest insight in the cascade is structural, and Roger Martin states it bluntly: where-to-play and how-to-win are inseparable. "A WTP choice is useless without a HTW for that WTP. Likewise, a HTW is useless without a WTP on which to focus." This is the antidote to the most common strategy-process error — picking a market first ("we're going after mid-market") and then, as a separate later exercise, figuring out how to win in it. Martin's point is that the two choices are a single choice: a market you can't win in is not a market you've chosen well, and a winning formula with no arena to deploy it is a fantasy. You select the pair together or you haven't selected a strategy.

For a product leader, the cascade is most useful as a completeness check on an existing strategy. A strategy missing its winning aspiration has no ambition to calibrate against; one missing capabilities has no honest account of whether it can actually be executed; one missing management systems will quietly revert because nothing reinforces the new choices. The five questions are less a template to fill in order than a set of holes to probe — and in practice the where-to-play / how-to-win pair is where most product strategies are weakest, because teams treat market selection as a sizing exercise rather than a winnability bet.

The DHM model — Biddle's product-specific test

Where Rumelt and Martin are general-purpose strategists, Gibson Biddle (ex-Netflix) offers the most quotable product-specific definition. The DHM model reduces product strategy to a single question: "How will your product delight customers in hard-to-copy, margin-enhancing ways?" The elegance is that it fuses three concerns product teams usually consider separately — customer love, defensibility, and business viability — into one test that a feature or bet must pass on all three axes simultaneously.

Each letter rules out a familiar failure. Delight (D) keeps the strategy honest about whether customers actually care, which kills internally-motivated roadmaps. Hard-to-copy (H) is the moat question — a delightful feature a competitor can ship next quarter buys you nothing durable, so it forces the strategy toward proprietary data, network effects, brand, or scale advantages rather than easily-cloned UI. Margin-enhancing (M) ties delight back to the business, ruling out the beloved-but-unprofitable feature that quietly erodes unit economics. A bet that hits two of three is the seductive trap: delightful and hard-to-copy but margin-destructive (many free consumer features), or hard-to-copy and margin-enhancing but joyless (lock-in tactics customers resent).

DHM pairs naturally with the harder-edged moat frameworks below: it tells you the shape of a good product bet, while Helmer's 7 Powers tells you which specific kinds of "hard-to-copy" actually persist under competition. Biddle's H is the question; Helmer's barriers are the rigorous answer.

Competitive advantage and moats — Helmer's 7 Powers

When the conversation turns to durable advantage, the canonical framework is Hamilton Helmer's 7 Powers, and its central definition is exacting. Per Sachin Rekhi's summary, a Power is the source of "persistent differential returns" — not just an advantage, but one that survives sustained competition. The two-part structure is what makes the framework rigorous: every Power has "two critical components: a benefit and a barrier." The benefit improves your economics (lower costs, higher prices, better retention); the barrier is what stops competitors from arbitraging that benefit away. A benefit without a barrier is not a moat — it is a head start that will be competed to zero.

The seven Powers — scale economies, network economies, counter-positioning, switching costs, branding, cornered resource, and process power — give a product strategist a checklist for where durable advantage can actually come from. Most product teams over-rely on the weakest of them. Features are not a Power (instantly copyable). A slick UI is not a Power. The Powers that product can genuinely build are network economies (each user makes the product more valuable to others), switching costs (data, workflow, and integration lock-in), and over time scale and process power. Counter-positioning — adopting a model the incumbent can't copy without damaging its own business — is the one that explains most successful disruptions and is the most underused lens in product planning.

The reason 7 Powers matters for strategy specifically, rather than just competitive analysis, is that it disciplines the how-to-win choice. "We'll win on product quality" is not a strategy because quality has no barrier; a competitor can match it. "We'll win by building a two-sided network whose value compounds with each cohort, creating switching costs no new entrant can match at our scale" is a how-to-win with a barrier attached. Helmer forces the strategist to name the barrier, which is exactly the part wishful strategies omit.

Situational awareness — Wardley maps

Most strategy frameworks help you choose; Simon Wardley's mapping helps you see first, on the premise that you cannot choose well in terrain you haven't mapped. Per the technique, "A Wardley map is a map for business strategy" that plots a value chain — user need at the top, the components that serve it arranged beneath by how visible they are to the user — against an evolution axis running from genesis to custom-built to product/rental to commodity/utility. The result is a literal map of where each component in your business sits and, crucially, where it is heading.

The strategic payload is that components evolve rightward over time — today's custom-built differentiator becomes tomorrow's commodity — and that movement is largely predictable. A strategy that treats a commoditizing component as a durable moat is building on sand; one that anticipates the shift can position ahead of it (commoditize a layer to undercut incumbents, or move up the value chain before your differentiator becomes a utility). Wardley's own framing of the value is modest and precise: the worth "lies in exposing assumptions." A map makes explicit the beliefs about position and evolution that teams otherwise argue about implicitly, which is why mapping is as much a tool for resolving strategy disagreements as for forming strategy.

Wardley maps are less widely adopted than Playing to Win or the kernel, partly because they require more upfront effort and a tolerance for ambiguity in placing components. But for product leaders operating in fast-evolving technical landscapes — infrastructure, AI tooling, platforms — they are the canon's best answer to the question the other frameworks assume away: how do you know where you are before you decide where to play?

North Star metrics and outcomes over output

Strategy fails most often not in the choosing but in the connecting — the gap between a strategy on a slide and the day-to-day work of a team. The outcomes camp exists to close that gap, and its first move is to attack output as the unit of progress. Teresa Torres draws the line precisely: "An output is something you build or produce — a feature, a project, an initiative," while "An outcome is the impact of that output — a change in customer behavior or a business result." Her one-sentence indictment of feature-factory culture is "Building something isn't the same as creating value." Josh Seiden gives the working definition the whole camp now uses — an outcome is "a change in human behavior that drives business results" — and the reframe that operationalizes it: stop asking "What should we build?" and start asking "What will people be doing differently as a result of this feature?"

The North Star Framework (associated with Sean Ellis, Amplitude, and John Cutler) is the most common mechanism for wiring a strategy to an outcome: a single metric that is customer-centric, a leading indicator of revenue, and a measure of the value customers actually receive, supported by a small set of inputs a team can move. The framework's most-cited discipline is also a warning — naming several metrics as "North Star Metrics" is "a misunderstanding"; the entire point is exactly one. The North Star is the bridge: it translates the strategy's diagnosis ("our challenge is shallow engagement") into a number a team can organize around without being handed a feature list.

At the front of the pipeline, Amazon's working-backwards process is the canon's sharpest tool for forcing a customer-grounded vision before a single feature is committed. Per the PR/FAQ process, teams write the press release and FAQ for a product before building it, because "writing a press release is a forcing function to ensure that the creator of the new product idea is focused on the customer." It moves the normally-last step to the front, so the team must commit to a crisp, customer-facing articulation of the value — a testable vision — before the strategy and roadmap that serve it are drawn. Together these three — outcomes, a North Star, and working-backwards — are how the camps that distrust grand strategy documents nonetheless do strategy: from the customer and the behavior change inward, rather than from the slide outward.

Common failure modes

The canon's failure modes rhyme, and naming them is half the defense. The first and most pervasive is strategy-as-roadmap: presenting a dated feature list as the strategy, which skips Rumelt's diagnosis and guiding policy entirely and leaves the team executing without a theory of why. The tell is that the document answers "what and when" but never "what's the central obstacle and what's our approach to it."

The second is strategy-as-goals — Rumelt's "mistaking goals for strategy" — where a stack of targets (grow X, improve Y) stands in for a strategy that explains how those targets will be reached against real resistance. The third is fluff: a vision-shaped paragraph of buzzwords that makes no falsifiable claim and could be signed by any competitor. The fourth, Perri's build trap, is the operational consequence of the first three — when the "strategy" is a feature plan, the organization measures output, and a feature factory is the inevitable result.

A subtler failure is the where-to-play without how-to-win error Martin warns against — choosing a market as a sizing exercise without an honest account of how you'll actually win in it. And the moat illusion: claiming durable advantage from something with no barrier, which Helmer's benefit-plus-barrier test exists specifically to catch. Each failure mode maps to a framework that diagnoses it, which is the practical reason to know more than one framework: they are lenses for spotting different ways the same strategy can be hollow.

Choosing an approach — which framework, when

The frameworks are not competitors; they answer different questions, and a fluent product leader reaches for the one that fits the gap. When the problem is that nobody can say what the strategy is, start with Rumelt's kernel — force a diagnosis, a guiding policy, and coherent action, and most of the confusion resolves. When the strategy exists but feels incomplete or keeps drifting, run it through Playing to Win's five choices as a completeness check and pressure-test the where-to-play / how-to-win pair.

When the question is whether a specific product bet is worth making, Biddle's DHM is the fastest filter — delightful, hard-to-copy, margin-enhancing, all three. When the question is whether an advantage will last, escalate to Helmer's 7 Powers and name the barrier explicitly. When you suspect the team is arguing about a landscape it hasn't actually mapped — especially in fast-evolving technical markets — Wardley mapping surfaces the hidden assumptions before you choose. And when the strategy is sound but not reaching the team, the outcomes toolkit — a North Star plus outcome-framed goals — is the connective layer.

The meta-skill is sequencing: diagnosis first (kernel), structure second (cascade), defensibility third (DHM/7 Powers), situational awareness woven through (Wardley), and outcomes as the delivery mechanism. Most strategy disasters come from skipping the first step and over-investing in the later ones — a beautifully structured strategy aimed at a misdiagnosed challenge is just a well-organized way to fail.

Live debates — where the canon disagrees

Several questions in product strategy are genuinely contested, and treating them as settled is itself a strategic error. The first: how prescriptive should a strategy be? Cagan and Perri lean toward strategy as living context that empowers teams to choose, while more traditional Playing-to-Win practice produces a tighter, more directive set of choices. Practitioners disagree on where the line sits between "enough guidance to align" and "so much that you've eliminated the team's judgment," and the right answer is genuinely context-dependent on team maturity and organizational stakes.

The second: OKRs versus a North Star metric as the anchoring mechanism. The North Star camp favors a single durable metric with supporting inputs; the OKR tradition favors time-boxed objectives that change quarterly. The two can coexist but often compete for the same role, and there is no consensus on which should sit on top — this remains an active, unresolved debate among the same practitioners who agree on outcomes over output. The third, related question is whether strategy should be expressed quantitatively at all, or whether over-metricizing strategy invites exactly the gaming the metrics were meant to prevent.

The third: top-down vision versus emergent strategy. Amazon's working-backwards is unapologetically top-down — commit to the destination, then work back — while the Wardley and lean-influenced traditions treat strategy as something that should adapt as the map reveals itself. And a quieter debate runs underneath all of them: whether these frameworks are genuinely generative or merely post-hoc rationalizations that make sense of strategy after the fact more than they produce it. A senior product leader's job is not to resolve these debates universally but to know which posture fits the situation in front of them — and to flag, when presenting a strategy, which contested choice it has quietly made.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

Strategy sits at the center of a web of adjacent product disciplines; each of these is a worthwhile next hop.

  • Product vision & storytelling — the destination strategy serves; how to write a vision that is inspiring yet testable (working-backwards, the PR/FAQ as a vision artifact).
  • Prioritization frameworks — RICE, WSJF, and cost-of-delay are how a strategy's guiding policy becomes an ordered backlog; the layer directly beneath strategy.
  • OKRs & goal-setting — the operational mechanism for translating strategy into team commitments, and one pole of the live debate above.
  • Continuous discovery — Torres's opportunity-solution trees are how outcome-framed strategy gets executed week to week.
  • Business models & unit economics — the margin-enhancing axis of DHM and the economic outcomes Perri's strategy connects vision to.
  • Competitive analysis & market positioning — the where-to-play choice and the raw material for a Wardley map.

References

  1. lennysnewsletter.com ·cited· (T1)

    Lenny Rachitsky's distillation of Rumelt: the kernel is diagnosis, guiding policy, coherent action, and "the most common cause of bad strategy is a weak diagnosis" — a strategy that doesn't honestly name the central challenge can't address it. A strategy is not a goal, a vision, or a list of priorities; it is a way through a specific obstacle.

  2. fs.blog ·cited· (T1)

    Lafley and Martin's Playing to Win frames strategy as "a coordinated and integrated set of five choices: a winning aspiration, where to play, how to win, core capabilities, and management systems" — "strategy is about making specific choices to win in the marketplace." Strategy is "an integrated set of choices that uniquely positions the firm in its industry so as to create sustainable advantage and superior value relative to the competition."

  3. rogermartin.medium.com ·cited· (T1)

    Roger Martin's strategy choice cascade is five linked choices — winning aspiration, where to play, how to win, capabilities, management systems — and the where-to-play (WTP) and how-to-win (HTW) choices are inseparable: "A WTP choice is useless without a HTW for that WTP. Likewise, a HTW is useless without a WTP on which to focus." Strategy is choosing both together, not picking a market then figuring out winning.

  4. melissaperri.com ·cited· (T1)

    Melissa Perri argues good product strategy is "a system of achievable goals and visions that work together to align the team around desirable outcomes" — not a plan. "This isn't a strategy, this is a plan... when we treat a product strategy like a plan, it will almost always fail." Strategy is a deployable decision-making framework, not a Gantt chart of features.

  5. mindtheproduct.com ·cited· (T1)

    Marty Cagan distinguishes vision from strategy: "Vision is describing your destination," typically "five to 10 years out," while product strategy is "a living thing" you keep "revisiting it every quarter." Empowered teams need strategic context to make good choices: "In order to make good choices... they need that strategic context." Vision inspires; strategy sequences the path to it.

  6. gibsonbiddle.medium.com ·cited· (T1)

    Gibson Biddle's DHM model defines product strategy as the answer to one question: "How will your product delight customers in hard-to-copy, margin-enhancing ways?" Delight (D) drives engagement, hard-to-copy (H) is the durable moat, and margin-enhancing (M) ties it to the business. A feature that delights but is trivially copyable, or is unique but unprofitable, is not strategy.

  7. sachinrekhi.com ·cited· (T1)

    Hamilton Helmer's 7 Powers defines a Power as the source of "persistent differential returns" — sustained advantage that survives competition. Every Power has "two critical components: a benefit and a barrier" — the benefit improves economics, the barrier prevents competitors from arbitraging it away. The seven: scale economies, network economies, counter-positioning, switching costs, branding, cornered resource, and process power.

  8. producttalk.org ·cited· (T1)

    Teresa Torres distinguishes outputs from outcomes: "An output is something you build or produce — a feature, a project, an initiative," while "An outcome is the impact of that output — a change in customer behavior or a business result." Her core principle: "Building something isn't the same as creating value." Outcome-assigned teams get autonomy to discover solutions; output-assigned teams just execute a feature roadmap.

  9. intercom.com ·cited· (T1)

    Josh Seiden defines an outcome as "a change in human behavior that drives business results." The reframe for teams: instead of asking "What should we build?", ask "What will people be doing differently as a result of this feature?" because "just shipping features and moving on is a huge problem for product teams" — a feature that ships but changes no behavior created no value.

  10. alexmurrell.co.uk ·cited· (T2)

    Rumelt's kernel of good strategy is three parts: a diagnosis that defines or explains the nature of the challenge by "comprehending the situation and identifying the biggest barriers"; a guiding policy "Like the guardrails on a highway" that channels action without dictating every move; and coherent actions that are coordinated to carry out the guiding policy. Rumelt's four hallmarks of bad strategy: fluff (gibberish masquerading as strategic concepts), failure to face the challenge, mistaking goals for strategy, and bad strategic objectives.

  11. t-ziegelbecker.medium.com ·cited· (T2)

    Perri's Escaping the Build Trap: the build trap is "when organizations measure their success by outputs rather than outcomes" — shipping features instead of creating value. Product strategy is "not a plan but a framework that helps to make decisions" and it "connects vision to economic outcomes." A product-led organization "is a culture that organizes around outcomes."

  12. romanpichler.medium.com ·cited· (T2)

    Roman Pichler frames a hierarchy: vision → strategy → roadmap → backlog, where decisions "become more specific" and time frames compress "from five to ten years covered by the vision" down to a backlog of "the next few months." The vision "describes the product's purpose, the ultimate reason for creating it, and the positive change it should bring about"; the strategy "communicates the approach chosen to realise the vision and to make the product successful" via choices about customer needs, target market, differentiators, and business goals.

  13. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Simon Wardley's mapping technique: "A Wardley map is a map for business strategy" that plots a value chain (user need at top, components beneath) against evolution (genesis → custom-built → product/rental → commodity/utility). Its purpose is situational awareness — "the value [lies] in exposing assumptions" about where components sit and where they are evolving, so strategy responds to the landscape rather than opinion.

  14. productcompass.pm ·cited· (T2)

    The North Star Framework (Sean Ellis, Amplitude, John Cutler) centers a single metric that is customer-centric, a leading indicator of revenue, and a measure of the value customers get. A common misuse is naming several metrics "North Star Metrics" — "a misunderstanding"; the framework's discipline is exactly one North Star supported by a small set of inputs the team can move.

  15. workingbackwards.com ·cited· (T2)

    Amazon's Working Backwards is "a systematic way to vet ideas and create new products" whose principal tool is the PR/FAQ (press release + FAQ). Writing the press release first "is a forcing function to ensure that the creator of the new product idea is focused on the customer" — normally the last step, moved to the front so the team commits to a customer-facing vision before building. It operationalizes "start with the customer and work backwards."

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_product-strategy-and-vision.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
